questions.question: If the Nominal GDP for a country is \$3,000 billion and the Real GDP is \$2,500 billion, calculate the GDP deflator for that year.
questions.explanation: GDP Deflator = (Nominal GDP ÷ Real GDP) × 100 = (\$3,000B ÷ \$2,500B) × 100 = 120. Prices are 20% higher than the base year.

questions.question: Calculate the Real GDP of an economy that produced \$800 billion in current dollars during a year when the GDP deflator was 160.
questions.explanation: Real GDP = Nominal GDP ÷ (GDP Deflator ÷ 100) = \$800B ÷ 1.60 = \$500 billion.

questions.question: In the expenditure approach to GDP, which component accounts for the change in private inventories held by businesses?
questions.explanation: Inventory changes are part of Investment (I). When firms add to inventories, it is counted as investment spending because those goods represent current production not yet sold to final consumers.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the effect on the GDP deflator if the prices of all domestically produced final goods and services increase by 10%?
questions.explanation: The GDP Deflator is a measure of the prices of domestically produced goods. If all those prices rise by 10%, the Deflator rises by 10% as well.

questions.question: Which type of unemployment refers specifically to workers who are currently in between jobs as they search for positions that better match their skill sets?
questions.explanation: Frictional unemployment is the short-term unemployment that occurs when workers voluntarily leave jobs to search for better matches. It reflects the normal functioning of a dynamic labor market.

questions.question: Which specific type of unemployment is caused by a decline in total spending in the economy, typically during a recession?
questions.explanation: Cyclical unemployment results from insufficient aggregate demand during economic downturns. It rises during recessions and falls during expansions.

questions.question: In a labor force consisting of 200,000 people, if 184,000 are currently employed, what is the official unemployment rate?
How does the Bureau of Labor Statistics classify an individual who is not working and has not looked for work in the past four weeks?
options: As not in the labor force
questions.explanation: To be counted as unemployed, a person must be actively seeking work. Someone who hasn't looked in four weeks is classified as not in the labor force.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the primary difference between the basket of goods used for the CPI versus the goods included in the GDP deflator?
options: CPI includes all goods; GDP deflator includes only consumer goods
CPI includes consumer goods with imports; GDP deflator includes only domestic goods
Both cover exactly the same set of goods
GDP deflator is always a subset of CPI
questions.explanation: CPI includes goods and services bought by households (including imports). The GDP Deflator covers all domestically produced final goods and services (excluding imports, including exports and capital goods).

questions.question: Which of the following would cause a rightward shift in the Aggregate Demand (AD) curve due to a change in consumer expectations?
options: Expectations of higher future interest rates
questions.explanation: If consumers expect higher future income, they feel wealthier and increase current consumption. AD shifts right as spending rises at every price level.

questions.question: Which of the following events would cause a sudden leftward shift in the Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS) curve, known as a negative supply shock?
options: A sharp increase in global energy prices
An increase in the labor force
questions.explanation: A surge in energy prices raises production costs across the economy. Firms supply less output at every price level, shifting SRAS left—a negative supply shock.

questions.question: In the AD-AS model, what is the result of a rightward shift in the Aggregate Demand curve regarding the equilibrium price level and Real GDP?
options: Price level falls and Real GDP falls
Price level rises and Real GDP rises
Price level rises and Real GDP falls
Price level falls and Real GDP rises
questions.explanation: A rightward AD shift increases total spending. In the short run, the new equilibrium has both a higher price level and higher Real GDP.

questions.question: Which curve must shift to the left to return the economy to long-run equilibrium if it is currently experiencing an inflationary gap?
questions.explanation: An inflationary gap means output exceeds potential. Tight labor markets drive wages up, increasing costs and shifting SRAS leftward until output returns to potential.

questions.question: Which of the following identifies a recessionary gap on an AD-AS graph?
options: AD-AS intersection to the right of LRAS
AD-AS intersection to the left of LRAS
AD curve above the LRAS curve
questions.explanation: A recessionary gap exists when the short-run equilibrium is to the left of LRAS. Actual GDP is below potential, and unemployment exceeds the natural rate.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the actual unemployment rate and the natural rate of unemployment when there is an inflationary gap?
options: Actual unemployment equals the natural rate
Actual unemployment exceeds the natural rate
Actual unemployment is below the natural rate
questions.explanation: In an inflationary gap, Actual GDP > Potential GDP, so labor demand is high. The actual unemployment rate falls below the natural rate.

questions.question: Which economic concept suggests that nominal wages do not adjust downward quickly during a recession due to labor contracts and morale?
questions.explanation: Downward nominal wage rigidity means wages are slow to fall due to long-term contracts, minimum wage laws, and worker morale. This prevents quick labor market adjustment during recessions.

questions.question: Which phase of the business cycle follows a 'Trough' and is characterized by increasing employment and rising Real GDP?
questions.explanation: After the Trough (lowest point), the economy enters an Expansion (recovery). Real GDP rises, unemployment falls, and economic activity accelerates.

questions.question: If the Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) is 0.72, what is the corresponding Marginal Propensity to Save (MPS)?
questions.explanation: MPS = 1 – MPC = 1 – 0.72 = 0.28. So 28% of each additional dollar of disposable income is saved.

questions.question: Calculate the Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) if a \$5,000 increase in national income leads to a \$3,800 increase in aggregate consumption.
questions.explanation: MPC = ΔConsumption ÷ ΔIncome = \$3,800 ÷ \$5,000 = 0.76. So 76% of additional income is consumed.

questions.question: Which of the following is defined as the level of consumption that would occur even if disposable income were zero?
questions.explanation: Autonomous consumption is the intercept of the consumption function—spending that occurs independent of income, financed by savings, borrowing, or wealth.

questions.question: Given the consumption function C = 100 + 0.8Y, calculate the level of Real GDP (Y) at which aggregate saving is exactly zero.
questions.explanation: S = Y – C = Y – (100 + 0.8Y) = 0.2Y – 100. Set S = 0: 0.2Y = 100 → Y = \$500. At this income level, all income is consumed.

questions.question: What is the government spending multiplier if the Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) is 0.8?
questions.explanation: Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC) = 1 ÷ 0.2 = 5. Each dollar of government spending generates \$5 of total income.

questions.question: Using an MPC of 0.75, calculate the total impact on Real GDP of a \$20 billion increase in personal income taxes.
questions.explanation: Tax multiplier = –MPC ÷ (1 – MPC) = –0.75 ÷ 0.25 = –3. ΔGDP = –3 × \$20B = –\$60 billion.

questions.question: Which of the following is a discretionary fiscal policy action intended to increase Aggregate Demand?
options: Progressive income taxes collecting more revenue during an expansion
Unemployment benefits automatically rising during a recession
A new government infrastructure spending program
Welfare payments adjusting with economic conditions
questions.explanation: Discretionary fiscal policy requires new legislation. A new infrastructure program is a deliberate policy action, unlike automatic stabilizers that adjust without legislative action.

questions.question: Which of the following government actions would be classified as a contractionary fiscal policy?
options: Increasing government spending and cutting taxes
Reducing government spending and raising taxes
Increasing government spending with no tax change
Cutting taxes with no spending change
questions.explanation: Contractionary fiscal policy reduces AD by cutting government spending (direct reduction) and raising taxes (reducing disposable income). Both actions cool an overheating economy.

questions.question: Which of the following describes an expansionary monetary policy action by a central bank using open market operations?
options: Selling government bonds to commercial banks
Purchasing government bonds from commercial banks
Raising the target overnight interest rate
questions.explanation: Buying bonds injects reserves into the banking system. This expands the money supply and lowers interest rates—expansionary monetary policy via open market operations.

questions.question: How does a central bank's decision to increase the target overnight rate affect the money supply and investment spending?
options: Money supply expands and investment rises
Money supply growth slows and investment falls
Money supply is unaffected but investment falls
Money supply expands and investment is unchanged
questions.explanation: A higher overnight rate raises banks' cost of funds. Lending becomes more expensive, slowing money supply growth. Higher borrowing costs reduce business investment spending.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an automatic stabilizer that reduces the severity of a recession without new legislation?
options: A newly approved tax cut by Congress
A discretionary increase in defense spending
questions.explanation: Unemployment benefits automatically increase payouts when joblessness rises. This supports household income and cushions the fall in AD without requiring new legislative action.

questions.question: If the government increases spending by \$10 billion and increases taxes by \$10 billion, what is the resulting change in equilibrium Real GDP?
questions.explanation: The balanced budget multiplier equals 1. When G and T increase equally, the net effect on Real GDP equals the change in spending: +\$10 billion.

questions.question: Which term describes the fiscal condition where a government's annual expenditures exceed its annual tax revenues?
questions.explanation: A budget deficit occurs when government spending exceeds tax revenues in a given fiscal year. The deficit adds to the national debt.

questions.question: In a corridor system of monetary policy, which rate acts as the upper limit for the overnight interest rate?
questions.explanation: In a corridor system, the bank rate (discount rate) sets the ceiling. Banks will not borrow overnight above this rate since they can access funds directly from the central bank at this rate.

questions.question: Which of the following explains the transmission mechanism from a lower overnight rate to increased consumer spending?
options: Lower rates raise borrowing costs and reduce spending
Lower rates reduce commercial lending rates, making durable goods purchases cheaper to finance
Lower rates directly increase government transfer payments
Lower rates have no effect on consumer behavior
questions.explanation: A lower overnight rate reduces banks' cost of funds, which lowers rates on consumer loans. Cheaper financing encourages spending on durable goods, cars, and housing.

questions.question: How does a rise in the central bank's policy rate affect the interest rate on existing variable-rate mortgages?
questions.explanation: Variable-rate mortgages are tied to the prime rate, which closely follows the central bank's policy rate. When the policy rate rises, variable-rate mortgage payments increase.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the impact of an increase in domestic interest rates on the demand for the domestic currency in the forex market?
options: Demand for the domestic currency decreases
Demand for the domestic currency increases
Demand is unaffected by interest rates
Demand shifts to foreign currencies only
questions.explanation: Higher domestic rates attract foreign capital seeking better returns. Investors must buy the domestic currency to invest, increasing its demand in the forex market.

questions.question: What is the expected result for the domestic currency's value if the central bank raises the overnight rate while foreign interest rates remain constant?
options: The domestic currency becomes convertible to gold
questions.explanation: Higher relative domestic rates attract capital inflows. Increased demand for the domestic currency causes it to appreciate in value.

questions.question: Which of the following sequences correctly traces the effect of an expansionary monetary policy on Aggregate Demand?
options: Money supply falls → interest rates rise → AD shifts left
Money supply rises → interest rates fall → consumption and investment rise → AD shifts right
Money supply rises → interest rates rise → AD shifts left
Money supply falls → interest rates fall → AD shifts right
questions.explanation: Expansionary monetary policy increases the money supply, which lowers interest rates. Lower rates stimulate interest-sensitive consumption and investment, shifting AD rightward.

questions.question: Through which mechanism does a contractionary monetary policy primarily reduce the investment component of AD?
options: It increases government borrowing and crowds out private investment
Higher interest rates raise the cost of capital, making fewer projects profitable
It directly restricts the number of firms allowed to invest
Lower interest rates discourage saving, reducing funds for investment
questions.explanation: Contractionary policy raises interest rates. Firms compare project returns to the higher cost of borrowing; fewer investment projects clear the hurdle rate, reducing I.

questions.question: In the circular flow model, which of the following is categorized as an injection into the economy?
options: Government purchases of goods and services
questions.explanation: Injections add to the circular flow: Investment (I), Government spending (G), and Exports (X). Government purchases directly inject spending into the economy.

questions.question: Which policy mix would be most effective at slowing down an overheating economy while attempting to maintain a balanced budget?
options: Expansionary fiscal and expansionary monetary policy
Contractionary monetary policy with a balanced fiscal stance
Expansionary fiscal and contractionary monetary policy
Contractionary fiscal and expansionary monetary policy
questions.explanation: Contractionary monetary policy (higher rates) reduces AD to cool the economy without changing the budget. A balanced fiscal stance maintains the budget position while monetary policy does the anti-inflation work.

questions.question: Which of the following is illustrated by a movement upward along the Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC)?
questions.explanation: An upward movement along the SRPC shows higher inflation paired with lower unemployment. This results from an increase in Aggregate Demand.

questions.question: How does an increase in the Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS) curve impact the position of the Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC)?
options: Shifts SRPC downward/leftward toward the origin
Has no effect on the SRPC
questions.explanation: SRAS and SRPC are mirror images. A rightward SRAS shift (lower costs) means lower prices and higher output, which shifts the SRPC downward toward the origin: lower inflation at every unemployment rate.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the slope of the Long-Run Phillips Curve (LRPC)?
questions.explanation: The LRPC is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment. In the long run, there is no trade-off between inflation and unemployment.

questions.question: Calculate the percentage change in the real wage if the nominal wage increases by 3% while the inflation rate is 5%.
questions.explanation: Real wage change ≈ Nominal wage change – Inflation = 3% – 5% = -2%. Purchasing power falls by approximately 2%.

questions.question: Which of the following represents the adjustment of a nominal wage to maintain a constant real wage during a period of inflation?
options: A wage freeze with zero increase
A nominal wage increase at the same rate as inflation
A nominal wage increase at half the inflation rate
questions.explanation: To maintain constant purchasing power, the nominal wage must rise at the same rate as inflation. This is known as a cost-of-living adjustment (COLA).

questions.question: What is the name for the level of output where the actual unemployment rate is exactly equal to the natural rate of unemployment?
questions.explanation: Potential GDP (full-employment output) is the sustainable level of Real GDP when unemployment equals the natural rate—frictional plus structural unemployment only.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the output gap when Real GDP is \$620 billion and Potential GDP is \$600 billion?
questions.explanation: Output gap = Actual GDP – Potential GDP = \$620B – \$600B = +\$20 billion. A positive gap is an inflationary gap: output exceeds potential.

questions.question: If the nominal exchange rate is 1.10 and the ratio of domestic prices to foreign prices is 1.2, calculate the real exchange rate.
questions.explanation: Real exchange rate = Nominal rate × (Domestic P ÷ Foreign P) = 1.10 × 1.2 = 1.32. Domestic goods are relatively more expensive in real terms.

questions.question: How does a depreciation of the nominal exchange rate affect the real exchange rate, assuming price levels remain constant?
options: The real exchange rate depreciates by the same proportion
The real exchange rate is unchanged
The price levels must adjust to offset the change
questions.explanation: Real exchange rate = Nominal rate × Price ratio. If price levels are constant and the nominal rate depreciates, the real rate depreciates proportionally.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause the supply of a domestic currency to shift to the right in the foreign exchange market?
options: An increase in foreign purchases of domestic exports
An increase in domestic purchases of foreign imports
An increase in foreign investment into the country
A rise in domestic interest rates relative to foreign rates
questions.explanation: To buy foreign imports, domestic residents sell domestic currency to acquire foreign currency. This increases the supply of domestic currency in the forex market, shifting the supply curve right.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the effect of a domestic currency appreciation on the nation's imports and exports?
options: Exports become cheaper and imports become more expensive
Exports become more expensive and imports become cheaper
Both exports and imports become cheaper
Both exports and imports become more expensive
questions.explanation: A stronger domestic currency makes exports more expensive for foreign buyers (reducing exports) and imports cheaper for domestic consumers (increasing imports). Net exports fall.
